name: Naval Station Norfolk
tagline: The world's largest naval station and the heart of the Atlantic Fleet, on Hampton Roads.
_note: First Navy base. Facts verified from official + well-corroborated sources (cnrma.cnic.navy.mil, norfolknavalhousing.com, portsmouth.tricare.mil, DeCA/NEX, Wikipedia for the headline figures it cites to the Navy). BAH: full VA298 (Norfolk/Portsmouth) 2026 table, verified value-for-value against the official DoD 2026 BAH rate table (all locations, all pay grades, effective 2026-01-01) on 2026-06-24. Killer content (the Hampton Roads commute/tunnel decision) is drawn from cited commute figures. Medium-confidence flags: base coords (approx Sewells Point), representative off-base ZIP 23505, housing partner branding (Lincoln Military Housing per the official Norfolk site; nationally transitioning to Liberty Military Housing in some markets).

data.Fleet: Homeport for Atlantic Fleet carrier strike groups

sections.title: Mission & Identity
data: Naval Station Norfolk is the world's largest naval station, occupying Sewells Point where the Elizabeth River and the James meet the Chesapeake Bay. It is the center of gravity for the U.S. Atlantic Fleet.

The station supports more than 75 ships across 14 piers, from aircraft carriers and submarines to surface combatants and Military Sealift Command vessels, plus the air operations at the adjacent Chambers Field. For a huge share of the Navy, a Norfolk tour is simply part of the career.

sections.title: Which Side of the Water? The Hampton Roads Commute

data.subtitle: Hampton Roads is a cluster of cities wrapped around the water. For a sailor at Norfolk, where you live decides whether your daily commute includes a tunnel, and the tunnels here are famous for traffic.
intro: The base sits at Sewells Point in north Norfolk, on the 'Southside' of Hampton Roads. The region is really several cities knit together by bridges and tunnels, so the neighborhood you pick sets your commute for the entire tour.

The single biggest factor is the Hampton Roads Bridge-Tunnel (HRBT) on Interstate 64. It carries roughly 100,000 vehicles a day and can add anywhere from 30 to 90 minutes when traffic, weather, or an incident backs it up. Sailors stationed at Norfolk overwhelmingly live on the Southside to avoid crossing it daily. Living on the Peninsula, in Hampton or Newport News, makes the HRBT part of your routine.

recommendations.advice: Live in Norfolk itself. Neighborhoods like Ghent and Larchmont run about 5 to 15 minutes to the base with no tunnel.
Virginia Beach (Kings Grant, Great Neck) or Chesapeake (Western Branch) are roughly 20 to 30 minutes via I-64 and I-564, no tunnel crossing.
Hampton and Newport News can be more affordable, but a daily HRBT crossing is the longest and least predictable commute in the region. Many Norfolk sailors avoid it.
Hampton Roads is a deep, military-heavy housing market that turns over with every PCS season. A local agent who actually knows the tunnels is worth a lot here.

sections.title: Gates & Access
data: Visitors without a DoD ID need a visitor pass and a REAL ID-compliant ID, arranged through the installation's Visitor Control Center.
Gate hours and visitor requirements change, so confirm the current details on the official Naval Station Norfolk site before you go.

sections.title: On-Base Housing
data: On-base family housing at Naval Station Norfolk is privatized, operated by Lincoln Military Housing across about 10 neighborhoods sized to rank and family needs.
You start with the Navy Housing Service Center, which processes applications and refers you to the property manager. Reach the housing office at (757) 447-9025.

sections.title: Schools
data: There is no DoDEA district in Hampton Roads, so children attend the local civilian public schools. On and near the base, that is Norfolk Public Schools.

Because families spread across Norfolk, Virginia Beach, Chesapeake, Portsmouth, and Suffolk, and each city runs its own school division, your assigned schools depend entirely on your address. Confirm the division and campus for any neighborhood you are considering.

data.Address: 620 John Paul Jones Circle, Portsmouth, VA 23708
data.Distinction: Oldest naval hospital in the U.S. (since 1830)

data.Exchange: Navy Exchange (NEX) Norfolk, one of the largest in the Navy

intro: Whether to rent or buy is the biggest money decision of most PCS moves, and the answer is mostly about how long you'll be here and how steady the local market is.
rent: Your tour is short (about two years or less) or your orders feel uncertain.
You want time to learn the area before committing to a neighborhood.
You'd rather not gamble on resale timing when you PCS out.
Your BAH comfortably covers rent in the areas you'd consider.
buy: You expect to be here three or more years.
You want to use your VA loan and build equity instead of paying rent.
The market is steady and you could rent the home out later if needed.
You've found a neighborhood and commute you're confident about.
data.bottom_line: There is no universal right answer. Run your BAH against the rent and home prices above, weigh your tour length and the local market, and talk to a lender and an agent who work with military families before you commit.

intro: The first month at a new duty station is mostly logistics. Knocking these out early makes everything else easier.

steps.title: Line up temporary lodging
steps.text: Book on-base lodging or a short-term rental before you arrive. You can claim Temporary Lodging Expense (TLE) for a limited number of days while you house-hunt.

steps.title: In-process and update DEERS
steps.text: Report to your unit and finance, and make sure DEERS and ID cards are current for every family member so TRICARE and base access work.

steps.title: Start with the Housing Office
steps.text: Check on-base availability and get the housing office's read on off-base neighborhoods before you sign anything.

steps.title: Register your vehicle and sort access
steps.text: Handle base access and vehicle registration, and learn the visitor-pass process for guests. Bring your registration and insurance.

steps.title: Enroll the kids in school
steps.text: Contact the gaining school district or DoDEA early and bring records and immunizations. The School Liaison Office can help with the transfer.

steps.title: Set up healthcare
steps.text: Update your TRICARE region and enrollment so you can be seen at the local military treatment facility or network providers.

data.q: Which side of the water should I live on?
data.a: Most sailors stationed at Norfolk live on the Southside (Norfolk, Virginia Beach, Chesapeake) to avoid crossing the Hampton Roads Bridge-Tunnel daily. See the commute section above.

data.q: How bad is the tunnel traffic?
data.a: The HRBT can add anywhere from about 30 to 90 minutes when it backs up, which is why where you live matters so much here.
Lincoln Military Housing, across about 10 neighborhoods. Start with the Navy Housing Service Center.
Naval Station Norfolk is MHA VA298 (Norfolk/Portsmouth). Use the calculator above for your grade.

picks.name: Nauticus & Battleship Wisconsin
picks.blurb: A maritime science center on the Norfolk waterfront, home to the battleship USS Wisconsin.

picks.name: Chrysler Museum of Art
picks.blurb: A well-regarded art museum in Norfolk's Ghent district, free to enter.
